In this sense Descartes was an exemplary ‘Protestant’ of the generic Weberian type, 9 using the ascetic techniques of withdrawal, reflection and self-perfection for this-worldly purposes of truth and order. 10 But Descartes 8 One can here refer to another central concept in Voegelin’s approach, namely ‘differentiation of consciousness’. Importantly, however, Voegelin identified two types of differentiation, noetic and pneumatic, working through mind and soul, and with the decisive differentiations taking place in the Hellenic and Judaeo-Christian traditions respectively. Using a Voegelinian language, one might risk the formulation that the noetic differentiation (and its real-world effects) that follows from Cartesian thought dangerously predicates pneumatic undifferentiation. 9 In separating faith from reason Descartes was in fact reinforcing, at the epistemological level, a tendency initiated by the Protestant reformers: the separation between faith and action. 10 Perhaps not too much should be made of it, but The Society of Jesus was formed as the principal arm of the Counter-Reformation, and exactly in that quality it had taken on Liminality and the Modern 126 is not only the philosopher of modernity because he locates truth and knowledge in the self, and because of his meditative path to truth. Both moves have their historical significance but they had been made before. If those were the criteria, stoicism should easily be recognized as the first truly modern philosophy. Despite all the obvious affinities, we generally hesitate to make this claim. Why? The answer is that Descartes is the philosopher of modernity because he is the first to systematically apply ascetic techniques to work on mind instead of soul, and because he is the first to apply the powers so gained to achieve system and mathematical precision in the de-Divinized this-worldly realm. In his discussion of Descartes, Borkenau shows how Descartes managed to offer a proper solution to the anxieties of the age, not at the level of philosophical or spiritual substance, but rather at the level of the ‘mood’ [Stimmung] that underlies a world image. Descartes is the philosopher of modernity because he reduces human existence to a cognitive challenge of positive knowledge established through work on the self as mind. And it is in this sense that the modern world is indeed Cartesian to the core (Watson 2007: 3). Fear and Order: The Hobbesian Self and the Hobbesian Sovereign Whosoever looketh into himself ... (Hobbes, Leviathan) If we could conceive the world annihilated except one man to whom there would remain ideas or images ... (Hobbes, drafts for De Corpore, quoted in Tuck) Even if Cartesian thinking quickly became an object of dispute, and even if several of his ideas were later falsified, his formulation of the general epistemological problem of Western philosophy would remain. From an anthropological perspective, cause for further wonder ought to reside in the question of the precise nature of the historical moment at which the question could ever become so paramount and thus formulated: how can the thinking self be the basis of universal truth? How can we, from within our own resources, arrive at some justifiable secure knowledge of the world? Fichte would phrase the pre-occupation well when he defined the very task of philosophy as a task to discover ‘the absolutely many of the educational principles that had given the Reformation its formidable successes. This implied a heavy stress on the internalization of religion, a systematic and rule-bound conduct, and also the idea that each person, high or low, should be treated equally before God. In order to shape the will of the person, Ignatius of Loyola, the founder of the movement, applied a soldier-like discipline of minute control of behaviour and constant supervision to the Jesuit institutions, and it was in such an environment, at the Jesuit School La Flèche, that Descartes had his primary education (see Gaukroger 1995: 38–65). It has often been argued that Descartes adopted his stoic, dissecting morality and his pragmatic this-worldly orientation here (see for example Watson 2007: 65–77). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 127 unconditioned first principle of human knowledge’, a task he identified as the Vocation of Man (Fichte 1961). However, this search for first principles was equally important for modern political thought. Thomas Hobbes’ problem of order was indeed similar in kind: what plagued Hobbes was how to establish, in the midst of the reigning disorder, some absolutely unconditioned first principle, not only of human knowledge, but also of political rule and morality. 11 Moreover, Hobbes’ answers ran in certain important respects parallel to those offered by Descartes. Hobbes’ problem can be described in similar terms as those introduced above. Europe had been in a prolonged state of war for almost half a century. His political writings emerged ‘in a period of intense political turmoil’ (Dietz 1990a: 1). During the Thirty Years’ War, Hobbes saw how the schismatic churches shed blood with the intent of establishing a secular order on the basis of their inwardly-felt religious claims to truth. It was exactly this use of religious truth for political order that Hobbes found both erratic and dangerous. Hobbes’ fear of passionate religious inner-driven sentiments was understandable, considering the historical context and the Puritan revolutions in England. Different varieties of moral philosophy and transcendental truth kept defending different varieties of legitimate public order, and the result was bloody, to say the least. As Hobbes says in his 1641 Epistle dedicatory to De Cive, ‘the war of the sword and the war of the pens is perpetual’ (in Tuck and Silverthorne 1998: 5), and to Hobbes both types of war were causally related. There was a need to clean up the mess, to find some ground from where truth could be established in order for peace to prevail. This was Hobbes’ problem. Christianity had lost its function as a non-disputed civil theology; its powers had been exhausted, that much was clear. Something else was needed, and Hobbes claimed
